title: Chocolate layer cake
subtitle: Two deep cocoa sponges, filled and covered with ganache
excerpt: Two 20 cm cocoa sponges sandwiched and topped with a dark chocolate ganache. This is the chocolate layer cake that the Victoria sandwich is not: deeper in tin, darker in flavour, finished with ganache rather than a dusting of icing sugar.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the foundational weighted British cake structure (equal-parts creaming). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: chocolate cakes appear in later editions; the base method is the same four-quarters structure with cocoa substituted for a portion of the flour. The ganache is a French patisserie technique first documented in the mid-nineteenth century; no single public-domain text is the primary source, but the technique is standard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooled sponges unfilled, wrapped tightly in cling film, for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature for 2 hours before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponges the day before and store wrapped. Make the ganache on the day; it sets firmest at room temperature.

recipeTools.notes: Two of them, loose-bottomed, greased and lined.
A balloon whisk and a strong arm work fine.
Palette knife for spreading the ganache.

glossaryTerms.definition: An emulsion of hot cream poured over chopped dark chocolate. The ratio determines the texture: equal weights of cream to chocolate gives a pourable glaze; two parts chocolate to one part cream sets to a firm, sliceable finish.

content.text: The sponges use the same creaming method as a Victoria sandwich but with two changes: a portion of the flour is replaced by cocoa powder, and a little soured cream goes in with the eggs to keep the crumb moist. The cake bakes in two 20 cm tins at a slightly lower temperature than a plain sponge, because the cocoa browns faster than flour alone. Ganache goes on thick between the layers and over the top; no buttercream, no piped decoration.
All the sponge ingredients should be at room temperature before you start. Cold butter will not cream; cold eggs will split the batter. Plan an hour of warming time before you begin.
What you need
Method
Prepare the tins and heat the oven

Heat the oven to 175°C fan. Grease two 20 cm loose-bottomed tins and line the bases with circles of baking paper. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more — an oven thermometer takes the guessing out, and a slightly under-temperature oven is more damaging to a chocolate sponge than to a plain one (cocoa browns before it sets).
Cream the butter and sugar, 5 minutes

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar together for 4 to 5 minutes on medium-high with a hand mixer, until the mixture is pale, fluffy, and almost double in volume. This is the creaming method; the air whipped into the butter is the cake's primary rise.
Add the eggs and soured cream

With the mixer on medium, add {{eggs}} beaten eggs a tablespoon at a time. If the mixture shows signs of splitting, add a tablespoon of the weighed flour and beat for 10 seconds. Beat in {{vanilla-extract}} vanilla extract and then {{soured-cream}} soured cream; the soured cream is acidic and reacts with the baking powder to give the crumb a little extra lift and keep it moist.
Fold in the dry ingredients

Sift {{self-raising-flour}} self-raising flour, {{cocoa-powder}} cocoa powder, and {{baking-powder}} baking powder over the bowl. Fold in with a large spatula in a figure-of-eight motion: cut down through the centre, scrape the base, and lift over the top. Stop when no dry streaks remain. Every extra stroke after that point deflates the batter.
Divide and bake, 26–28 minutes

Weigh the batter and divide it equally between the two prepared tins — about 420 g per tin. Smooth the surface lightly and bake for 26 to 28 minutes. The sponges are done when the tops spring back to a fingertip touch and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. If the skewer comes out with wet batter, give it 2 more minutes and test again.
Cool

Leave the tins on a rack for 10 minutes. Turn the sponges out, peel off the paper, and leave to cool completely on the rack before filling. This takes at least 30 minutes; a warm sponge melts ganache.
Make the ganache

Place {{dark-chocolate}} finely chopped dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl. Heat {{double-cream}} double cream in a small saucepan until it just comes to a simmer; bubbles appear around the edge but the surface is not yet fully rolling. Pour the hot cream over the chocolate and leave without stirring for 2 minutes. Then stir from the centre outward in slow circles until the
ganache
is smooth and glossy. Equal weights of cream and chocolate give a ganache that sets to a firm, spreadable texture at room temperature.
Assemble

Sit one sponge flat-side-up on a serving plate. Let the ganache cool for 20 to 30 minutes at room temperature until it holds its shape when you lift a spoonful; it should mound rather than run. Spread half the ganache over the base sponge, going to within 5 mm of the edge. Place the second sponge on top, flat-side-down. Spread the remaining ganache over the top and smooth it with a palette knife. The ganache will set firmer over the next hour.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: The four most common problems with this cake.

items.symptom: Sponges sank or have a dense, tight crumb
items.cause: Butter and sugar not creamed long enough, or the batter was over-folded after the flour went in.
items.fix: Cream for the full 4 to 5 minutes until pale and doubled in volume. Stop folding the moment the dry ingredients are incorporated.

items.cause: Over-baked, or too little soured cream.
items.fix: Check the sponge at 26 minutes. Pull it as soon as the skewer comes out clean — an extra 2 minutes on a cocoa sponge dries it noticeably.

items.cause: Used immediately after making, before it has had time to cool and thicken.
items.fix: Leave the ganache at room temperature for 20 to 30 minutes. If the kitchen is warm, 10 minutes in the fridge helps; watch it closely and stir every 5 minutes.

items.cause: Cream was too hot when poured, or the chocolate was stirred too aggressively before the two had emulsified.
items.fix: Steep without stirring for 2 minutes after pouring the cream; then stir from the centre outward slowly. If it has split, add a tablespoon of lukewarm water or cream and stir gently from the centre.

content.text: Where this cake sits

Chocolate cake in Britain follows the same structural logic as any layer sponge: the creaming method, equal-weighted tins, a filling between the layers. The cocoa sponge became a staple in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries as cocoa powder became reliably available. The ganache is a French addition; British versions traditionally used chocolate buttercream, which is cheaper and more stable in warm weather. Either works for this recipe; ganache produces a darker flavour and a thinner coat.
